publicationStmt.p: Transcribed from Bodleian Library, Lovelace Byron collection, Box 166, folios 95-98. Transcription by Lisa Gold (2022). TEI conversion for Sextant pipeline, 2026.

div.p: St James' Sqre Weddy-- 10th April 1 o'clock
I have thought of you with no small distress, dearest W--, & I feel I have not one word to say for myself. I hope you will not fail to see me on Friday, before you go to Ashley.

I have had no intention, I can truly & honestly say, of teasing & hurting you. The consequence was that when I found I had (most undesignedly) had those effects, I felt beyong measure exasperated with you for having felt hurt.-- I am not pleading for the justice or fairness of this. Far from it. I merely mention it as a
fact.
Now one word about the children. I shall make it a rule henceforward that they always come to spend a little time with you, previous to any absence you are to make.
You may depend on me for this. Had I known your wish in good time yesterday, they would have been all ready for you to see comfortably & at leisure.
You must allow me to point out to you that you do not always give sufficient notice of your wishes & orders.

You cannot imaging, for instance, the inconvenience of a sudden announcement that you want to have the children down. It has not be expected, & perhaps it occurs while a master or other appointment is

going on. The child gets ready with what haste he or she can; but still it is probably a little after date; which inconveniences you, puts you out of temper, & then follows disagreements & all sorts of mischief.
Had I but known yesterday, you might have had the three children, (& heard that masterly music also, & enjoyed it thoroughly), all quite in early time & at ease.

I must endeavour to impress on you the great importance of telling me things beforehand. If they are distasteful to me, I then at least am able to make them less so, or even agreeable, by arrangement.
I do not feel I am fairly dealt with in this. Either thro'

negligence or intention on your part, you give me no notice of anything scarcely. I then find suddenly some arrangement made of which I knew nothing, & which consequently happens often to be at direct variance with some unwitting plan of my own. But I am expected to make no objections & manifest no surpise, however impromptu the thing may be, & however unpleasant for what I had planned.

I can safely promise you that a little more forethought & notice on your part, would most materially alter everything you now compain of. You would find things & tastes of yours consulted & provided for, as if by magic, & without any trouble to yourself.
It is my first wish, dearest Mate, to make your happiness

& comfort; but I assure you it is not easy; simply because you give me no helping hand. I know this is from diffidence on your part, to a certain extent. But it is a diffidence very pernicious to our mutual happiness, & which makes my game a difficult one.

I am glad to have the occasion of stating this to you clearly, in writing. You will get it tomorrow morning I hope. A line by return of post would be a great relief to me. And say in it when you are likely to return on Friday, what time you wish dinner &c.
Now here is just an occasion when you would probably inform me of nothing in a usual way. The consequence would be I might be out or engaged; & you think what an indifferent unfeeling wife you
had. Whereas she could not know by intuition when to expect you, or what you would wish; (& ten to one I should have preferred being tout a [accent mark over the a] vous, to what I might be doing).

I should always prefer being with you to any plans of my own. But then I must have notice, of course. And sometimes it will occur that other engagements are of real importance to me, or indirectly to yourself, & must command me in preference.

I have bought a thrush. Only 18 pence he is. The poor fellow took my fancy in the street. He has a large wicker cage, & lives in the School-room. I thought you too, would be pleased with me for this.--
I am very busy, with my music, & with looking after the young, who go on nicely.
I rose at 7 this morning, & practised for one hour before breakfast. Dr L-- wishes me to get up at seven; & also advises me to practise a good deal at present.

I do not neglect the riding & walking. But as I find the sun affects my head too much in the middle of the day, & do not like just now to ride at the fashionably late hour, I take my 2 chestnuts to the Riding-School instead, on hot days like this. The expense is next to nothing, as I do not require the master's presence or us his horses; & it enables me to derive healthy & effective exercise, & does the horses great good. Tag Rag
has a corn, which was why he stumbled. I insisted on his being examined without his shoes. It is not much, but he is better for riding-school ground just for a few days.
So I hope you will approve altogether; & do believe me your own nice Bird, my Dear.
Pray write to me, if only 6 words. I am quite miserable about you, because I know you are unhappy, & fancy you have an unkind bird.
